content_en:
Abu al-Fadl Iyad ibn Musa ibn Iyad al-Yahsubi as-Sabti, universally known as Qadi Iyad, was born in 476 AH in Ceuta (Sabta), the North African port city opposite Gibraltar. He rose to become one of the greatest scholars of the Maliki tradition and one of the most celebrated figures in the history of Islamic scholarship across all schools, known not only for his extraordinary erudition but for his personal piety and his fearless defense of Islamic principles in a politically turbulent era. He served as qadi (judge) in Ceuta and later in Granada, and his judicial career ran alongside his prodigious scholarly output.

Qadi Iyad is best known in the broader Islamic world for his Ash-Shifa bi-Tarif Huquq al-Mustafa — The Cure by the Knowledge of the Rights of the Chosen One — a celebrated work on the love of the Prophet and the obligations that love entails, which has been one of the most widely read books in the Islamic tradition for nine centuries. But among scholars of hadith and jurisprudence, his Ikmal al-Mu'lim bi-Fawa'id Muslim — Completing the Teacher's Work with the Benefits of (Sahih) Muslim — is equally important as one of the most significant early commentaries on the collection of Imam Muslim.

The title Ikmal al-Mu'lim refers to Qadi Iyad's completion and expansion of an earlier partial commentary on Sahih Muslim by the Maliki scholar al-Maziri, known as al-Mu'lim bi-Fawa'id Muslim. Al-Maziri's work was important but did not cover the entire Sahih Muslim, and Qadi Iyad undertook the considerable task of completing and in many cases revising and expanding it to produce a full commentary on Imam Muslim's collection. The resulting work is one of the major achievements of Andalusian and North African Maliki scholarship.

Qadi Iyad died in 544 AH in Marrakech, where he had been exiled in his final years by the Almohad rulers. His death under politically difficult circumstances added to the aura of scholarly martyrdom that surrounded his memory and contributed to the reverential reception his works received in subsequent generations.
